title: Spending Adjustment Test
skillName: Adjusting spending to close small gaps
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: When you last had a small money shortage, did you first try to adjust expenses or immediately think about borrowing?
Which 2–3 flexible expenses in your life could you temporarily reduce if a small gap appears?

stages.prompt: You are short by ₹1,000 this month. Which is the safer first step?

options.label: Reduce non‑essential spending and see if the gap closes
options.reflection: Correct. For small gaps, adjusting wants and flexible expenses is usually safer than rushing to borrow.

options.label: Take an instant app loan for ₹1,000
options.reflection: For small, regular gaps, instant loans can quickly become a habit and add extra interest and fees.

options.label: Delay rent or EMI and pay late
options.reflection: Delaying critical payments can damage your credit and may lead to penalties or conflict with lenders or landlords.

options.label: Immediately borrow from friends every time this happens
options.reflection: Depending on others regularly can strain relationships and still does not fix the root cause.

stages.prompt: Which habit best prepares you to adjust spending when needed?

options.label: Never tracking small daily expenses
options.reflection: If you do not know where small amounts go, it is hard to know what to adjust in a crisis.

options.label: Tracking categories like food, travel, and entertainment
options.reflection: Yes. Category tracking makes it easier to see where cuts are possible without touching essentials.

options.label: Checking expenses only when there is already a big gap
options.reflection: Waiting until the gap is big reduces your options. Early awareness gives you more control.

options.label: Blaming low income without looking at spending
options.reflection: Income can be a challenge, but thoughtful spending is still an important part of financial control.
